---

author: "Nicky Wakim"

    data-background-color: "#3070BF"

    chalkboard: true

    show-slide-number: all

## Why Does File Organization Matter?

Good organization means you can:

- **Find files** without hunting
- **Re-run your analysis** months later
- **Share your work** without thorough explanations
- **Avoid broken paths** and missing files
- **Collaborate** without stepping on each other

## Folder organization

Every analysis project should live in **one folder** that contains everything needed to reproduce it.

::: columns
::: column
For a project, I have the following folders

::: {.large-code}
```
project_name/ 
├── admin/
├── background/
├── code/
├── data/
│   ├── data_info/
│   ├── data_raw/          #<1> 
│   └── data_processed/    
├── dissemination/
├── meetings/
├── output/
└── README.md
```
:::

1. Original data — never modify this
:::

::: column
-   For our class, I suggest making one folder for the course with the following folders in it:

    -   Data
    -   Homework
    -   Lessons
    -   R_activities
    -   And other folders if you want
:::
:::

-   Take a few minutes to create these folders


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}

```{=html}
<countdown-timer class="countdown" id="timer_ce81b2c6" minutes="5" seconds="0" update-every="1" tabindex="0" style="right:0;bottom:0;"></countdown-timer>
```

:::
:::


## Aside: folder and file naming

There are a few good practices for naming files and folders for easy tracking:

1. Keep the name short and relevant
2. Use leading numbers to help organize sequential items
    - I can show you my lessons folders as an example 
3. Use dates in the format "YYYY-MM-DD" so that files are in chronological order
4. You can label different versions if you would like to 
5. Use "_" to separate sections of the name
    - I also use this to separate words, but some people say you should use "-" to separate words

## The Core Principle: Self-Contained Projects

Every analysis project should live in **one folder** that contains everything needed to reproduce it.

::: {.large-code}
```
my_analysis/         ← Everything lives here
├── data/
├── scripts/
├── outputs/
└── README.md
```
:::

If you moved this folder to another computer, it should still work.

::: {.callout-warning}
**Avoid** scattering files across your Desktop, Downloads, and Documents. Future you will not remember where anything is.
:::

---

## A Standard Folder Structure

```
my_project/
├── data/
│   ├── raw/          # Original data — never modify this
│   └── processed/    # Cleaned, transformed data
├── scripts/          # Your R scripts
├── outputs/
│   ├── figures/      # Plots and charts
│   └── tables/       # Summary tables, exports
├── docs/             # Reports, notes, references
└── README.md         # What this project is and how to run it
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
You don't need every folder for every project — but **`data/raw/`**, **`scripts/`**, and **`outputs/`** are almost always worth having.
:::

---

## Raw Data is Sacred

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Never modify raw data files.**

Treat `data/raw/` as read-only:

- Load it in R
- Clean and transform in a script
- Save results to `data/processed/`
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```r
# Good workflow
raw <- read.csv("data/raw/survey.csv")

cleaned <- raw |>
  filter(!is.na(age)) |>
  mutate(age_group = cut(age, breaks = c(0, 30, 60, 100)))

write.csv(cleaned,
          "data/processed/survey_clean.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)
```
:::
:::

. . .

If you ever need to start over, your original data is untouched.

---

## Naming Files: The Rules

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Avoid ❌**

```
Final version.R
analysis REVISED.R
data (2).csv
my script v3 FINAL2.R
Script for Sarah.R
untitled.R
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Prefer ✅**

```
01_clean_data.R
02_exploratory_analysis.R
03_model_fitting.R
survey_responses_2024.csv
species_counts_raw.csv
README.md
```
:::
:::

---

## Naming Conventions: Three Rules

**1. Use lowercase letters and underscores (or hyphens)**

```
# Good
clean_data.R        survey-responses.csv

# Avoid
Clean Data.R        Survey Responses.csv    cleanData.R
```

. . .

**2. Be descriptive, but concise**

```
# Good           # Too vague    # Too long
03_model.R       script3.R      third_script_for_the_linear_model_analysis.R
```

. . .

**3. Use numbers to indicate order**

```
01_import_data.R
02_clean_data.R
03_visualize.R
04_model.R
```
This keeps files sorted correctly in any file browser.

---

## Naming Data Files

Include relevant context in the filename itself:

```
# Who / What / When / Version
participants_baseline_2024.csv
sensor_readings_site_A.csv
climate_monthly_1990_2023.csv
```

. . .

::: {.callout-tip}
**Dates in filenames:** Use `YYYY-MM-DD` format (ISO 8601).  
`report_2024-03-15.pdf` sorts correctly; `report_15-03-24.pdf` does not.
:::

. . .

**Avoid version-tracking in filenames:**

```
# Avoid this — use version control (Git) instead
analysis_v1.R   analysis_v2_final.R   analysis_FINAL_forreal.R
```

---

## Absolute vs. Relative File Paths

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Absolute path ❌**
```r
read.csv("/Users/jane/Desktop/
          my_project/data/raw/
          survey.csv")
```
Breaks on any other computer (or even after moving the folder).
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Relative path ✅**
```r
read.csv("data/raw/survey.csv")
```
Works as long as you're working from the project root.
:::
:::

. . .

::: {.callout-important}
**Rule of thumb:** If a file path starts with `/` or `C:\`, it's absolute and likely to break for anyone else.
:::

We'll cover R Projects and `here::here()` in the next lesson — they make relative paths easy and automatic.

---

## Organizing Your Scripts

Split your analysis into **numbered, single-purpose scripts**:

| Script | Does |
|---|---|
| `01_import_data.R` | Loads raw data, minimal changes |
| `02_clean_data.R` | Filtering, recoding, joining |
| `03_explore.R` | Summary stats, exploratory plots |
| `04_model.R` | Statistical modeling |
| `05_figures.R` | Final publication-ready plots |

. . .

Each script should be **runnable on its own** (given the right inputs exist), and should **save its outputs** to a known location.

---

## The README File

Every project folder should have a `README.md` at the top level.

```markdown
# Survey Analysis — Spring 2024

## What this project does
Analyzes student survey responses collected in March 2024.

## Data
- `data/raw/survey_2024.csv` — Original export from Qualtrics
- `data/processed/survey_clean.csv` — Cleaned version (run 01_clean.R)

## How to run
1. Open `my_project.Rproj` in RStudio
2. Run scripts in order: 01 → 02 → 03 → 04

## Dependencies
R 4.3+, tidyverse, ggplot2
```

---

## Quick-Reference Checklist

Use this when setting up a new project:

- [ ] Created a single top-level project folder
- [ ] Subfolders: `data/raw/`, `data/processed/`, `scripts/`, `outputs/`
- [ ] Raw data is untouched in `data/raw/`
- [ ] Scripts are numbered in run order
- [ ] Filenames are lowercase with underscores, no spaces
- [ ] No absolute file paths in scripts
- [ ] `README.md` explains the project

---

## Summary

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
**The key habits:**

1. **One project = one folder** — keep everything together
2. **Protect raw data** — never overwrite originals
3. **Name files clearly** — lowercase, underscores, numbered
4. **Use relative paths** — so code works anywhere
5. **Write a README** — for collaborators (and yourself)
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
Good organization is a gift to your future self.

The five minutes you spend now saves hours of confusion later.
:::
:::

. . .

**Up next:** R Projects and working directories — the tools that make all of this automatic.

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
